---
title: Incomplete or Undisclosed PRIADs Are Invalid Tools for Causal Inference
author:
- Quentin André
date: '2026-05-20'
description: PRIADs that are not correctly pre-registered, or that are not disclosed in the paper, will inflate false-positive rates. We illustrate this issue using two recent marketing papers.
categories: [Peer-Review]
toc: false
draft: true
---

When Nick Reinholtz and I wrote our Pre-Registered Interim Analysis Design (PRIAD; Andr&eacute; and Reinholtz 2024) paper, we wanted to give researchers the ability to adopt more flexible designs in their research, and to save valuable resources.

However, we were also concerned that this additional flexibility would result in too many false-positives, which is why we wrote in the paper that every PRIAD should be accompanied with a pre-registration specifying:

* That a PRIAD will be used
* The number and timing of the peeks
* The type of correction (O'Brien-Fleming or Pocock)
* The resulting critical *p*-values at each peek

Following the publication of our paper, I have been checking how researchers are embracing PRIADs, and noticed that these guidelines are not always followed. In this post, I will discuss two papers that fall in this category, and explain what they should have done differently.

## Szocs et al. (2024, Journal of Consumer Psychology)

The pre-registration of Study 3 ([AsPredicted #179174](https://aspredicted.org/8HC_TYY)) mentions: "We will recruit up to 600 US based Prolific panelists. Following the PRIAD approach (Andre and Reinholtz 2024), we will conduct two interim analyses at N = 200 and N = 400". This pre-registration is not precise enough: It mentions the number and timing of the peeks, but the type of correction (Pocock vs. O'Brien-Fleming) is not specified, and the resulting *p*-values are not disclosed.

The issue does not only lie in the pre-registration. The write-up of the paper says "We requested 200 US-based Prolific panelists and received 199 completed surveys". The mention of 200 observations suggest that researchers conducted a peek at N = 200 and stopped data collection there, but the paper never mentions interim analyses, sequential testing, or PRIAD at all.

Fortunately, these two omissions are without consequences: Since the *p*-value of this study is below the conservative O'Brien-Fleming alpha threshold (0.00052), the result would hold anyway.

So in this case (i) the PRIAD is incomplete, (ii) the results silently deviate from the pre-registration, but at least the statistical conclusion are still valid.

However, things do not always turn up so favorably.

## Kim et al. (2026, Journal of Marketing)

Kim et al. (2026) make two errors across the **six** studies that mention a PRIAD in the pre-registration (i.e., Study 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 4c):

1. The pre-registrations specify the number and timing of the peeks, but do not mention the correction or the resulting critical *p*-values.
2. The write-ups of the studies do not mention that a PRIAD was used. The sample sizes suggest that the PRIADs were indeed used (the numbers sometimes correspond to the first peek, sometimes to the last), but the results are reported as if the study was a *fixed N* design.

Unlike in Szocs et al. (2024), the silent omissions of the PRIADs are consequential: Many of the results reported in the paper would **not** be significant with an O'Brien-Fleming correction.

The table below summarizes, for each study, the pre-registered design, the sample size reported in the paper, the main hypothesis test, and the O'Brien-Fleming (OBF) critical *p*-value for the PRIAD peek that the reported sample size corresponds to.

| Study | Pre-reg                                          | PRIAD # of Peeks (k)        | Paper N              | Peek Reached | Main hypothesis test          | OBF critical *p* | Robust to OBF? |
| ----- | ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------ | ----------------------------- | ---------------- | -------------- |
| 2     | [#251407](https://aspredicted.org/9aj6mc.pdf)    | N = [200, 300], k = 2       | 189 (200 recruited)  | 1st of 2     | χ²(1) = 9.48, *p* = .002      | 0.014            | Yes            |
| 3a    | [#185369](https://aspredicted.org/3s4f-pbw4.pdf) | N = [400, 600, 800], k = 3  | 805                  | 3rd of 3     | Wald χ²(1) = 3.16, *p* = .075 | .043             | **No**         |
| 3b    | [#236425](https://aspredicted.org/vrjz-ht6s.pdf) | N = [500, 1000], k = 2      | 846 (1000 recruited) | 2nd of 2     | Wald χ²(1) = 5.63, *p* = .018 | .048             | Yes            |
| 4a    | [#235978](https://aspredicted.org/mn9h-3nq9.pdf) | N = [500, 1000], k = 2      | 429 (501 recruited)  | 1st of 2     | Wald χ²(1) = 5.83, *p* = .016 | .005             | **No**         |
| 4b    | [#179021](https://aspredicted.org/nms7-m4n9.pdf) | N = [400, 600, 800], k = 3  | 750 (800 recruited)  | 3rd of 3     | Wald χ²(1) = 3.00, *p* = .083 | .043             | **No**         |
| 4c    | [#274736](https://aspredicted.org/5zf54q.pdf)    | N = [400, 800, 1200], k = 3 | 387 (400 recruited)  | 1st of 3     | Wald χ²(1) = 3.90, *p* = .048 | .0005            | **No**         |

To summarize:

* Four of the six pre-registered main hypothesis tests do not clear the O'Brien-Fleming threshold for the timing they stopped data collection at. 
* Three of them (Studies 3a, 4b, 4c) also fail under the more lenient Pocock correction (constant boundary *p* = .0245 for k = 3, *p* = .0294 for k = 2). 
* Study 4c is the clearest case: the paper reports *p* = .048 at N = 387 as a "significant" interaction. However, since this sample size corresponds to the *first* of three pre-registered PRIAD peeks, the *p*-value would need to be smaller than .0005 (assuming an O'Brien-Fleming correction) or than .022 (assuming a Pocock correction). The reported *p*-value exceeds both thresholds.

So, as with Szocs et al. (2024): (i) the PRIAD specification is incomplete, (ii) the paper does not mention the PRIADs. This time, however, the statistical conclusions are also affected: multiple "significant" results in the paper would not survive the pre-registered interim-analysis correction.

## What to do instead: A reminder

If you use PRIAD (or any sequential design):

1. **Pre-register the correction**, not just the number and timing of the peeks. You can do that easily in the [PRIApp](https://priadconsumerresearch.shinyapps.io/PRIApp/), which will then automatically compute and report the critical *p*-value at each look. The pre-registration should make the inferential rule completely unambiguous — given the data, a third party should be able to reproduce the stop/continue/declare-significant decision without consulting you.
2. **Disclose the procedure in the paper.** State that the study used a sequential design, name PRIAD (or whatever procedure you used), report the number and timing of the peeks, report the critical values, and report which peek produced the final sample. This belongs in the methods section of the paper.

The point of pre-registration is to take researcher discretion out of the inferential pipeline. The point of writing methods clearly is to let readers evaluate the inference. A PRIAD that is only mentioned only in a pre-registration, and only with the looks but not the critical values, does not accomplish these goals.
